cataloging_subject.label: The Holy Bible, Containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal Books
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is the exact four-volume edition-level compilation published by Josiah Forshall and Frederic Madden in 1850 and represented by University of Michigan item AFZ9170.0001.001. It coordinates the Earlier and Later Versions of the Middle English Bible in parallel, with prefatory epistles, the Later Version General Prologue, additional prologues, a table of lessons, and an extensive witness apparatus. It is one publication set, not 196 DIV1 elements, 6,405 PB occurrences, 3,314 page images, a single medieval manuscript, a single undifferentiated Bible translation, or the complete oeuvre of John Wyclif. The medieval version traditions, individual manuscript witnesses, 1850 editorial layer, U-M page-image and OCR layers, and checked-in XML remain distinct in the lineage record.

catalog_summary.title: The Holy Bible, Containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal Books
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English translators and revisers; historically attributed in varying parts to John Wyclif, Nicholas Hereford, and John Purvey
catalog_summary.date: Earlier Version probably 1370s; Later Version revision probably 1380s; edition published 1850
catalog_summary.region: Probably Oxford, England; edition published at Oxford

titles.value: The Holy Bible, Containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal Books
notes: The preferred title is the edition title, shortened before its historical responsibility phrase.

titles.value: The Holy Bible, Containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal Books, in the Earliest English Versions Made from the Latin Vulgate by John Wycliffe and His Followers
notes: The phrase assigning the versions to Wyclif and his followers is preserved as 1850 title-page evidence, not adopted as a modern authorship conclusion.

titles.value: Middle English Bible: Earlier and Later Versions

assertion.notes: A conventional scholarly name retained for discovery; it does not settle personal authorship or a uniformly Lollard origin and reception.

agents.name: Anonymous Earlier Version translators
notes: The surviving version is collaborative and textually layered; no complete named translator roster is established.

agents.name: Anonymous Later Version revisers and translators
notes: The Later Version revises the Earlier Version toward more idiomatic prose while continuing to consult the Latin Vulgate tradition.

agents.name: John Wyclif
notes: Personal authorship of either complete Bible version is not accepted as established.

agents.name: Nicholas of Hereford
notes: A note in MS Douce 369 associates him with the Earlier Version through Baruch 3:20, but the scope and force of that note remain debated.

agents.name: John Purvey
notes: Forshall and Madden assigned the Later Version and General Prologue to Purvey; modern scholarship does not accept the attribution as demonstrated.

agents.name: Jerome
notes: Traditional author of the Latin prefatory epistles translated in the edition; not every transmitted biblical prologue bearing his name is necessarily authentic.

assertion.notes: The high confidence attaches to the fact that the attribution is disputed, not to Wyclif's authorship.

notes: Forshall and Madden's title assigns the versions to Wyclif and his followers; modern summaries reject confident personal attribution.

scope.description: Earlier Version Old Testament through Baruch 3:20

notes: A manuscript attribution is significant evidence but does not establish sole translation of the entire scoped text.

assertion.notes: The high confidence attaches to rejection of the once-confident attribution as unproven.

notes: This responsibility concerns the Latin source epistles, not the anonymous Middle English translation.
This is the publication date of the cataloged edition-level compilation, not the medieval translations' composition date.
The edition was published at the Oxford University Press; this does not prove the place of every medieval component's translation.

scope.description: Forshall and Madden's title matter, headings, apparatus explanations, and other nineteenth-century editorial matter
Latin source readings, titles, and formulae embedded in the English edition and apparatus

form_statements.description: The biblical verse numbers are prose segmentation milestones; the XML contains no L or LG verse-form elements.

subject_statements.label: The Bible in Middle English translation
Textual transmission, witness variation, and collation
Authorship, attribution, and the construction of the Wycliffite corpus

extent.description: One four-volume publication set whose XML serializes nine GROUP/TEXT streams: four Earlier Version streams and five Later Version streams. The file has 10 TEXT, 9 BODY, 1 GROUP, 196 DIV1, 2,858 DIV2, 48 DIV3, 2,942 P, no L or LG, 98,765 NOTE1, 671 NOTE2, 6,405 PB occurrences with 3,314 unique REF values, and 72,418 MILESTONE elements. The repeated PB occurrences mark positions in parallel serialization; the header's 3,314 TIFF images are the historical page-image carrier extent.

content_parts.label: Forshall–Madden four-volume publication set
content_parts.summary: The single edition-level cataloging unit, coordinating two Middle English translation-version streams and their editorial apparatus across four volumes.

content_parts.label: Encoded edition title matter
content_parts.summary: The U-M XML verbally encodes the 1850 title page while most other preliminary pages are represented only by page milestones.

content_parts.label: Earlier Version
content_parts.summary: The more literal late-fourteenth-century Middle English version, represented in four serialized text streams and printed in parallel with the Later Version.

locators.value: Earlier Version parallel columns, volumes I–IV

content_parts.label: Earlier Version: Jerome's prefatory epistles
content_parts.summary: The prefatory epistles transmitted before the Earlier Version Old Testament.

content_parts.label: Earlier Version: Old Testament and Apocryphal books
content_parts.summary: Forty-seven book-level DIV1 units from Genesis through II Maccabees, with 1,104 DIV2 and 24 DIV3 units and extensive variant notes.

content_parts.label: Earlier Version: New Testament
content_parts.summary: Twenty-seven book-level DIV1 units from Matthew through Apocalypse, with 285 DIV2 units and extensive variant notes.

content_parts.label: Table of lessons, epistles, and Gospels
content_parts.summary: A liturgical table transmitted with Earlier Version manuscripts and constructed by the editors from Corpus Christi College Oxford MS 4 and British Library Add MS 15580, with six further copies collated.

content_parts.label: Later Version
content_parts.summary: The more idiomatic late-fourteenth-century revision, represented in five serialized text streams and printed in parallel with the Earlier Version where both contain the same biblical books.

locators.value: Later Version parallel columns and paratext, volumes I–IV

content_parts.label: Later Version: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: The sixteen-chapter General Prologue, historically assigned to John Purvey but anonymously transmitted and not securely attributable to him.

content_parts.label: Later Version: Jerome's prefatory epistles
content_parts.summary: The Later Version prefatory epistles placed before Genesis, taken by the editors from New College Oxford MS 66.

content_parts.label: Later Version: Old Testament and Apocryphal books
content_parts.summary: Forty-seven book-level DIV1 units from Genesis through II Maccabees, with 1,115 DIV2 and 24 DIV3 units and extensive variant notes.

content_parts.label: Later Version: New Testament
content_parts.summary: Twenty-eight book-level DIV1 units, including the Epistle to the Laodiceans, with 288 DIV2 units and extensive variant notes.

content_parts.label: Later Version: additional prologues
content_parts.summary: Additional prologue material arranged under twenty-four book groupings in the XML; the edition draws this material from several manuscript and transcript sources rather than one continuous codex.
notes: Current identities for every 1850 source designation behind these additional prologues remain unresolved and are not collapsed into a single base witness.

content_parts.label: Forshall–Madden textual apparatus
content_parts.summary: An integrated apparatus reporting manuscript variants, corrections, omissions, glosses, and source sigla throughout both version streams.

locators.value: 98,765 NOTE1 and 671 NOTE2 elements distributed through the nine text streams

content_parts.label: Page-linked but untranscribed front and back matter
content_parts.summary: The XML retains PB milestones for seventy-two pages labeled omitted front matter and fifty-four pages labeled omitted back matter, but no verbal OCR for those wrappers.

locators.value: outer FRONT/DIV1[@TYPE='omitted front matter'] and BACK/DIV1[@TYPE='omitted back matter']

notes: Presence as PB milestones is not treated as verbal transcription.

summaries.value: Forshall and Madden's 1850 four-volume scholarly edition places the Earlier and Later Versions of the Middle English Bible in parallel and preserves a large manuscript-variant apparatus. The Earlier Version is generally more literal; the Later Version revises it into more idiomatic prose while continuing engagement with the Latin Vulgate. The set also includes prefatory epistles, the anonymous General Prologue, additional prologues, and a liturgical table of lessons.

The record describes a publication-defined compilation. Neither the parallel versions nor their apparatus are silently reduced to one abstract medieval work, and no single physical manuscript is treated as the source of the entire edition.

The U-M representation is uncorrected OCR with Level-1 TEI-style encoding and linked page images. Repeated PB elements occur in parallel serialization; 6,405 PB occurrences resolve to 3,314 unique image REF values, matching the header's historical image count.

related_works.title: Middle English Bible, Earlier Version
notes: A version-level intellectual entity distinct from the 1850 edition and from any one manuscript witness.

related_works.title: Middle English Bible, Later Version
notes: A revision/version-level intellectual entity distinct from Royal MS 1 C VIII, the 1850 edition, and the U-M encoding.

related_works.title: Latin Vulgate Bible textual tradition
notes: This relation names a textual tradition, not one demonstrated physical Latin exemplar.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML for afz9170
evidence.summary: Establishes repository identity, U-M item identifier, 1850 source-edition citation, 2006 uncorrected OCR and encoding statement, 3,314-image header extent, and the encoded text.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual inspection of afz9170 XML
evidence.locator: outer FRONT and BACK; GROUP/TEXT 1–9; element counts; PB REF and SEQ inventories
evidence.summary: Verifies the nine-stream boundary, book and chapter structure, prose form, variant-apparatus element counts, omitted-matter wrappers, 6,405 PB occurrences, and 3,314 unique page-image references.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, The Holy Bible, containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal books
evidence.summary: Confirms stable item identity, 1850 publication, publicly searchable full text, current public-domain / No Copyright–United States assertion, and page-view links from text sections.
notes: Automated direct requests received HTTP 403, but the current indexed item and section pages were retrievable through web search.

evidence.citation: Josiah Forshall and Frederic Madden, eds., The Holy Bible, Containing the Old and New Testaments, with the Apocryphal Books, vol. I (Oxford: University Press, 1850)
evidence.locator: title page; preface; manuscript table; General Prologue and parallel Genesis–Ruth text
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for edition title and responsibility, translation and attribution history as understood in 1850, editorial method, principal source and collated manuscripts, and volume-I contents.

evidence.citation: Josiah Forshall and Frederic Madden, eds., The Holy Bible, vol. II (Oxford: University Press, 1850)
evidence.locator: parallel Earlier and Later Version text, I Kings–Psalms
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the second-volume boundary and parallel biblical text.

evidence.citation: Josiah Forshall and Frederic Madden, eds., The Holy Bible, vol. III (Oxford: University Press, 1850)
evidence.locator: parallel Earlier and Later Version text, Proverbs–II Maccabees
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the third-volume boundary and parallel biblical text.

evidence.citation: Josiah Forshall and Frederic Madden, eds., The Holy Bible, vol. IV (Oxford: University Press, 1850)
evidence.locator: parallel Earlier and Later Version New Testaments; additional prologues; table of lessons; glossary and errata
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the fourth-volume boundary, New Testament streams, additional prologues, table, and terminal editorial matter.

evidence.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, “Bible Translation and Controversy in Late Medieval England,” in A Companion to Medieval Translation (Arc Humanities Press, 2019), 51–62
evidence.summary: Supports neutral Middle English Bible terminology, the broad 1370s/1380s dating, the literal-to-fluent distinction between versions, and the present non-acceptance of confident Wyclif, Hereford, and Purvey attributions.

evidence.citation: Mary Dove, The First English Bible: The Text and Context of the Wycliffite Versions (Cambridge University Press, 2007)
evidence.locator: excerpt; manuscript sigla; index of manuscripts; discussion of Forshall–Madden's base text and attribution limits
evidence.summary: Documents the large and varied manuscript tradition, the edition's side-by-side versions, the lack of evidence for Purvey's authorship of the General Prologue, the composite translation process, and the fact that Royal MS 1 C VIII is partly a revised Later Version rather than a transparent universal witness.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries medieval-manuscripts catalog records for MSS Douce 369, Douce 370, Corpus Christi College Oxford 4, and New College Oxford 66
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmarks, dates, contents, Forshall–Madden sigla and source scopes, Douce 369's two-part contents and Hereford note, and New College 66's mixed Earlier/Later Version prologue material.
notes: The Corpus Christi and New College records are present in the official Bodleian catalog repository's drafts area and do not yet expose MARCO ARKs.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog records for Royal MSS 1 B VI and 1 C VIII, Harley MS 1666, and Add MS 15580
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmarks, contents, dates, physical extents, the edition's use of Royal 1 C VIII as standard Later Version text, Harley 1666's General Prologue, and Add 15580's Earlier Version and prefixed table of lessons.

evidence.citation: Wycliffite Bible Digital Edition, Charles University
evidence.summary: Provides a current scholarly project description: a complete English Bible translation produced probably at Oxford by followers of John Wyclif near the end of the fourteenth century.

Records edition, version traditions, principal text-forming witnesses, U-M encoding and page-image layers, Internet Archive scans and derivatives, access, editorial practices, and layered rights evidence.

open_questions.question: What translator and reviser responsibilities can current specialist scholarship assign at book or passage level without reviving the superseded blanket Wyclif–Hereford–Purvey model?
next_steps: Review current book-level linguistic and textual studies; preserve manuscript attributions and modern reconstructions as qualified claims rather than names inherited from the 1850 title page.

open_questions.question: What are the exact current shelfmarks, survival states, and item-level catalog records for every manuscript and private source separately listed in Forshall and Madden's Earlier Version, Later Version, prologue, and table apparatus?
next_steps: Build a siglum-by-siglum concordance from the 1850 preface through Dove and Solopova, preserving each witness's exact transcription or collation scope; do not treat the apparatus census as one composite base.

open_questions.question: Can every source behind the volume-IV additional prologues be mapped to a current holding, especially the 1424 Granger manuscript known to the editors through Joseph Ames's transcript and the privately held Acland source?
next_steps: Trace the historical ownership and sale records and compare current manuscript indices before adding direct source relations.

open_questions.question: Is a stable complete download or auditable inventory currently exposed for the 3,314 U-M page images, and what is the exact current image-delivery format and size?
next_steps: Retry the page-view routes in an interactive browser or ask U-M Digital Collections; automated requests to image-view paths returned HTTP 403, so no bundle size or completeness claim was invented.

open_questions.question: What production decision determined which 1850 preliminary and terminal matter was represented only by PB milestones rather than OCR-transcribed text?
next_steps: Request U-M production documentation while retaining the directly observed encoded-versus-page-linked boundary.

notes: The XML header's generic language value 'eng' is not used to erase the Middle English language of the medieval version text.
Biblical verse numbers are semantic milestones in prose, not evidence of verse-form composition.
Royal MS 1 C VIII is the edition's continuous Later Version base but is not equated with a pure or universal Later Version: modern scholarship identifies revised Later Version strata within it.
The exact title-page phrase 'by John Wycliffe and his followers' is retained as edition history while current authorship remains conservative and scoped.
